title: Low-latency FPGA-based electronic control system for fast preparation of defect-free atom arrays
abstract: The scalability of neutral atom quantum computing demands integrated electronic control systems with low latency, modular architecture, and real-time feedback capability. Here, we present an FPGA-based electronic control system that eliminates the PC from the feedback loop, integrating photon counting, real-time decision-making, and waveform generation within a unified PXIe architecture. The system achieves a total feedback latency of $282\,\mathrm{μs}$ and is validated in practical experiments by assembling defect-free atom arrays from 24 stochastically loaded optical tweezers. A single-round rearrangement achieves a filling fraction of $\sim96\%$, while feedback-controlled iterative rearrangement over five rounds boosts the success probability for generating a 10-atom defect-free array from $65.7\%$ to $95.4\%$. This system establishes the electronic infrastructure necessary for mid-circuit measurement and real-time quantum error correction on neutral-atom platforms.

bite.hook: A new FPGA control system cuts feedback latency to 282 μs, boosting atom array assembly success to 95.4%.
tldr: They developed an FPGA-based electronic control system that removes the PC from the feedback loop to speed up neutral-atom quantum computing.
They integrated photon counting, real-time decision-making, and waveform generation within a unified PXIe hardware architecture.
They achieved a 282 μs feedback latency, raising the success rate of preparing a 10-atom defect-free array from 65.7% to 95.4% over five rounds.
bite.whyItMatters: Scaling up neutral-atom quantum computers requires incredibly fast, real-time control to correct errors and rearrange atoms before they lose their quantum states. This hardware-level feedback bypasses slow PC processing, clearing a major bottleneck for practical quantum error correction.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to arrange a grid of marbles on a shaking table using a robotic arm, but you can only see where they are through a slow camera. By putting the 'brain' of the camera directly inside the robotic arm's motor, you can react instantly to place the marbles perfectly before they roll away.

keyNumbers.label: feedback latency
keyNumbers.context: The total time taken by the FPGA system to count photons, make a decision, and generate a control waveform.

keyNumbers.label: success probability
keyNumbers.context: The probability of successfully generating a defect-free 10-atom array after five rounds of feedback-controlled rearrangement.

keyNumbers.label: initial success probability
keyNumbers.context: The baseline success rate for preparing the 10-atom array before applying the iterative feedback loop.

glossary.shortDef: Field-Programmable Gate Array; an integrated circuit designed to be configured by a customer after manufacturing.
glossary.eli5Def: A computer chip that can physically rewire its own internal connections on the fly to perform specific tasks incredibly fast.

glossary.shortDef: A quantum computing approach using uncharged atoms trapped by laser beams as qubits.
glossary.eli5Def: A way of building quantum computers where individual, neutral atoms are held in place by tiny tractor beams of light.

glossary.shortDef: PCI eXtensions for Instrumentation Express; a modular, high-performance PC-based platform for test and measurement.
glossary.eli5Def: A heavy-duty industrial plug-and-play system that lets different high-speed electronic boards talk to each other almost instantly.
